revision.comment: Created article, split off from the "Saio" article.
revision.text:
[[Image:Saiku_model.jpg|frame|right|A 1/10th scale model of Saiku (Meiwa Town, Mie Prefecture, Japan).]] {{nihongo|'''Saikū'''|斎宮|}} was a village that served as the palace and public offices of the [[Saiō]], an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the emperor from the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Nanboku-cho period]] of [[Japan]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, Saikū reverted to a rice-farming village that currently exists as a part of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa Town]], [[Mie Prefecture]].
==Saikū site layout==
The ancient Saikū site measures 2 kilometres from east to west, 700 metres from north to south and covers a total area of 137 hectares, making it one of the largest historical sites in Japan. Sections of the eastern administrative district of the site have already been excavated and working is currently being carried out in the site's central area. Individual walled blocks of 120 metres in length have been discovered, seperated by roads in a strict grid pattern.
==Saikū's Golden Period==
Saikū reached it's peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. Many buildings and relics that date from this period have been found. Buildings that date from this time are of the [[Shinden-zukuri]] style and were made of cedar and Japanese cypress and were constructed by interlocking wooden beams without the use of nails.
==Saikū today==
A museum stands today on the site where the first artifacts of the ancient Saikū were rediscovered around 1970 while digging foundations for new housing. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saiku train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing [[Shinden-zukuri]] buildings elsewhere in Japan.
==References==
Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience information broucher.
===External links=== *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/SAIKU/HP/ Saiku Historical Museum (Japanese)] *[http://www2.mint.or.jp/~itukino/ Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall (Japanese)]
[[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:Mie Prefecture]]

revision.comment: Moved the remainder of the information from the Saio article regarding Saiku here.
revision.text:
[[Image:Saiku_model.jpg|frame|right|A 1/10th scale model of Saiku (Meiwa Town, Mie Prefecture, Japan).]] {{nihongo|'''Saikū'''|斎宮|}}, also known as "Itsukinomiya" (いつきのみや), was a village located approximately 10 kilometers north-west of [[Ise Shrine]], arguably the most significant [[Shinto]] shrine in Japan. Sometimes referred to as the Bamboo Palace, Saikū served as the palace and public offices of the [[Saiō]], an unmarried Imperial princess who served at Ise Shrine on behalf of the emperor from the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Nanboku-cho period]] of [[Japan]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, Saikū reverted to a rice-farming village that currently exists as a part of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa Town]], [[Mie Prefecture]].
==Saikū site layout==
The ancient Saikū site measures 2 kilometres from east to west, 700 metres from north to south and covers a total area of 137 hectares, making it one of the largest historical sites in Japan. Sections of the eastern administrative district of the site have already been excavated and working is currently being carried out in the site's central area.
The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were buildings of varying size and purpose, built of Japanese cypress in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage.
==Saikū's Golden Period==
Saikū reached it's peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. Many buildings and relics that date from this period have been found. Buildings that date from this time are of the [[Shinden-zukuri]] style and were made of cedar and Japanese cypress and were constructed by interlocking wooden beams without the use of nails.
==Saikū today==
A museum stands today on the site where the first artifacts of the ancient Saikū were rediscovered around 1970 while digging foundations for new housing. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saiku train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing [[Shinden-zukuri]] buildings elsewhere in Japan.
==References==
Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience information broucher.
===External links=== *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/SAIKU/HP/ Saiku Historical Museum (Japanese)] *[http://www2.mint.or.jp/~itukino/ Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall (Japanese)]
[[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:Mie Prefecture]]

revision.text:
[[Image:Saiku_model.jpg|frame|right|A 1/10th scale model of Saiku (Meiwa Town, Mie Prefecture, Japan).]] {{nihongo|'''Saikū'''|斎宮|}}, also known as "Itsukinomiya" (いつきのみや), was a village located approximately 10 kilometers north-west of [[Ise Shrine]], arguably the most significant [[Shinto]] shrine in Japan. Sometimes referred to as the Bamboo Palace, Saikū served as the palace and public offices of the [[Saiō]], an unmarried Imperial princess who served at Ise Shrine on behalf of the emperor from the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Nanboku-cho period]] of [[Japan]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, Saikū reverted to a rice-farming village that currently exists as a part of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa Town]], [[Mie Prefecture]].
==Saikū site layout==
The ancient Saikū site measures 2 kilometres from east to west, 700 metres from north to south and covers a total area of 137 hectares, making it one of the largest historical sites in Japan. Sections of the eastern administrative district of the site have already been excavated and working is currently being carried out in the site's central area.
The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were buildings of varying size and purpose, built of Japanese cypress in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage.
==Saikū's Golden Period==
Saikū reached it's peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. Many buildings and relics that date from this period have been found. Buildings that date from this time are of the [[Shinden-zukuri]] style and were made of cedar and Japanese cypress and were constructed by interlocking wooden beams without the use of nails.
==Saikū today==
While digging foundations for new housing in 1970 in the Saikū area, a large [[Haniwa]] horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and excavations began, confirming the site of the ancient Saikū town. Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery.
Each year further excavations take place on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over it's long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at it's peak in size and influence.
In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing [[Shinden-zukuri]] buildings elsewhere in Japan.
==References== *Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience information broucher.
===External links=== *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/SAIKU/HP/ Saiku Historical Museum (Japanese)] *[http://www2.mint.or.jp/~itukino/ Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall (Japanese)]

revision.text:
[[File:Saiku model.jpg|frame|right|A 1/10 scale model of Saiku (Meiwa Town, Mie Prefecture, Japan).]] {{nihongo|'''Saikū'''|斎宮|}}, also known as "Itsukinomiya" (いつきのみや), was a village located approximately 10 kilometers north-west of [[Ise Shrine]], arguably the most significant [[Shinto]] shrine in Japan. Sometimes referred to as the Bamboo Palace, Saikū served as the palace and public offices of the [[Saiō]], an unmarried Imperial princess who served at Ise Shrine on behalf of the emperor from the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Nanboku-chō period]] of Japan. After the collapse of the Saiō system, Saikū reverted to a rice-farming village that currently exists as a part of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa Town]], [[Mie Prefecture]].
==Saikū site layout==
The ancient Saikū site measures 2 kilometres from east to west, 700 metres from north to south and covers a total area of 137 hectares, making it one of the largest historical sites in Japan. Sections of the eastern administrative district of the site have already been excavated and working is currently being carried out in the site's central area.
The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were buildings of varying size and purpose, built of Japanese cypress in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage.
==Saikū's Golden Period==
Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. Many buildings and relics that date from this period have been found. Buildings that date from this time are of the [[Shinden-zukuri]] style and were made of cedar and Japanese cypress and were constructed by interlocking wooden beams without the use of nails.
==Saikū today==
While digging foundations for new housing in 1970 in the Saikū area, a large [[Haniwa]] horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and excavations began, confirming the site of the ancient Saikū town. Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery.
Each year further excavations take place on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence.
In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing [[Shinden-zukuri]] buildings elsewhere in Japan.
==References== *Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience information brochure.
===External links=== *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/SAIKU/HP/ Saiku Historical Museum (Japanese)] *[http://www2.mint.or.jp/~itukino/ Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall (Japanese)]

revision.text:
[[File:Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg|thumb|Reconstructed halls of Saikū]] [[File:Saiku model.jpg|thumb|A 1/10 scale model of Saikū in Meiwa, Mie Prefecture]] [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] {{nihongo|'''Saikū'''|斎宮|}}, or ''Saigu'', also known as ''Itsuki-no-miya'' (いつきのみや), was a village located approximately 10 kilometers north-west of [[Ise Shrine]], arguably the most significant [[Shinto]] shrine in Japan. Sometimes referred to as the Bamboo Palace, Saikū served as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at Ise Shrine on behalf of the emperor from the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Nanboku-chō period]] of Japan. After the collapse of the Saiō system, Saikū reverted to a rice-farming village that currently exists as a part of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa Town]], [[Mie Prefecture]].<ref>http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm</ref>
== History == Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. Many buildings and relics that date from this period have been found. Buildings that date from this time are of the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style and were made of cedar and Japanese cypress and were constructed by interlocking wooden beams without the use of nails.
While digging foundations for new housing in 1970 in the Saikū area, a large [[Haniwa]] horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and excavations began, confirming the site of the ancient Saikū town. Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery.
Each year further excavations take place on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence.
In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.
== Site layout== The ancient Saikū site measures 2 kilometres from east to west, 700 metres from north to south and covers a total area of 137 hectares, making it one of the largest historical sites in Japan. Sections of the eastern administrative district of the site have already been excavated and working is currently being carried out in the site's central area.
The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were buildings of varying size and purpose, built of Japanese cypress in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage.
== References == {{Reflist}} *Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience information brochure.
== External links == {{commonscat-inline}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20060106075305/http://www.pref.mie.jp/saiku/hp/ Saiku Historical Museum (Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20080110232127/http://www2.mint.or.jp/~itukino/ Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall (Japanese)]

[[File:Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg|thumb|Reconstructed halls of Saikū]] [[File:Saiku model.jpg|thumb|A 1/10 scale model of Saikū in Meiwa, Mie Prefecture]] [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''', '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''|斎宮}},<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref> was a village located approximately 10 kilometers north-west of [[Ise Shrine]], arguably the most significant [[Shinto]] shrine in Japan. Sometimes referred to as the Bamboo Palace, Saikū served as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at Ise Shrine on behalf of the emperor from the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Nanboku-chō period]] of Japan. After the collapse of the Saiō system, Saikū reverted to a rice-farming village that currently exists as a part of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa Town]], [[Mie Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref>
== History == Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. Many buildings and relics that date from this period have been found. Buildings that date from this time are of the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style and were made of cedar and Japanese cypress and were constructed by interlocking wooden beams without the use of nails.
While digging foundations for new housing in 1970 in the Saikū area, a large [[Haniwa]] horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and excavations began, confirming the site of the ancient Saikū town. Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery.
Each year further excavations take place on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence.
In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.
== Site layout== The ancient Saikū site measures 2 kilometres from east to west, 700 metres from north to south and covers a total area of 137 hectares, making it one of the largest historical sites in Japan. Sections of the eastern administrative district of the site have already been excavated and working is currently being carried out in the site's central area.
The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were buildings of varying size and purpose, built of Japanese cypress in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage.
== References == {{Reflist}} *Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience information brochure.
== External links == {{commonscat-inline}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20060106075305/http://www.pref.mie.jp/saiku/hp/ Saiku Historical Museum (Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20080110232127/http://www2.mint.or.jp/~itukino/ Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall (Japanese)]
{{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=title}}

revision.text:
<noinclude>{{User:RMCD bot/subject notice|1=Saikū Palace|2=Talk:Saikū#Requested move 24 October 2023}} </noinclude>{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}}, was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[Taki District, Mie|Tai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saiku Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == {{commonscat-inline}} *[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/ Saiku Historical Museum] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.itukinomiya.jp Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall ] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.town.meiwa.mie.jp/main/soshiki/saikuuato/bunkazaik/saiku/1455610636852.html Meiwa Town home page] {{in lang|ja}}
{{Authority control}}

<noinclude>{{User:RMCD bot/subject notice|1=Saikū Palace|2=Talk:Saikū#Requested move 24 October 2023}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}

Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saiku Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saiku Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saiku Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

revision.text:
<noinclude>{{User:RMCD bot/subject notice|1=Saikū Palace|2=Talk:Saikū#Requested move 24 October 2023}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}}, was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Ise Saiō|Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saiku Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == {{commonscat-inline}} *[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/ Saiku Historical Museum] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.itukinomiya.jp Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall ] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.town.meiwa.mie.jp/main/soshiki/saikuuato/bunkazaik/saiku/1455610636852.html Meiwa Town home page] {{in lang|ja}}
{{Authority control}}

revision.text:
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

{{Italic title|reason=[[:category:kk]][[category:dd]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[category:kk]][[category:dd]][[category:kk]][[category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Italic title|reason=[[:category:kk]][[category:dd]][[category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Saikū]][[category:kk]][[category:dd]][[category:kk]][[category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Saikū]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Saikū]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Saikū]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Meiwa, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Nara period|Nara]] through [[Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[Nara period]] to the early [[Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[Saikū Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
