<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Saikū]][[es:Saikū]][[fa:سایکو (محوطه تاریخی)]][[fr:Saikū]][[ja:斎宮]][[sh:Saikū]][[zh-classical:齋宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q150845]] Wikidata name: Saikū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa, Mie]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[:en:Nara period|Nara]] through [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[:en:Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[:en:Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[:en:Saikū Station|Saikū Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yamada Line|Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[:en:Saikū Station|Saikū Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yamada Line|Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa, Mie]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[:en:Nara period|Nara]] through [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[:en:Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[:en:Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[:en:Saikū Station|Saikū Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yamada Line|Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa, Mie]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[:en:Nara period|Nara]] through [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Meiwa, Mie|Meiwa]], [[:en:Taki District, Mie|Taki District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Forming a small village, it was established in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[:en:Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into [[:en:Ise Bay|Ise Bay]]. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''[[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]]'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after [[:en:Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]]. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from [[:en:Saikū Station|Saikū Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yamada Line|Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|WD=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa, Mie}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} through {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|WD=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa}}, {{ill|Taki District, Mie|en|Taki District, Mie|WD=Q1042360|lt=Taki District}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Forming a small village, it was established in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[:en:Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''{{ill|Shinden-zukuri|en|Shinden-zukuri|WD=Q2975225|lt=Shinden-zukuri}}'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after {{ill|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|en|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|WD=Q728218|lt=Dazaifu}}. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from {{ill|Saikū Station|en|Saikū Station|WD=Q7400129|lt=Saikū Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yamada Line|Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|WD=Q20983066|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == {{commonscat-inline}} *[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/ Saiku Historical Museum] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.itukinomiya.jp Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall ] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.town.meiwa.mie.jp/main/soshiki/saikuuato/bunkazaik/saiku/1455610636852.html Meiwa Town home page] {{in lang|ja}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Saikū]][[es:Saikū]][[fa:سایکو (محوطه تاریخی)]][[fr:Saikū]][[ja:斎宮]][[sh:Saikū]][[zh-classical:齋宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Saikū]][[es:Saikū]][[fa:سایکو (محوطه تاریخی)]][[fr:Saikū]][[ja:斎宮]][[sh:Saikū]][[zh-classical:齋宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Saikū]][[es:Saikū]][[fa:سایکو (محوطه تاریخی)]][[fr:Saikū]][[ja:斎宮]][[sh:Saikū]][[zh-classical:齋宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q150845]] Wikidata name: Saikū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|WD=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa, Mie}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} through {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|WD=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa}}, {{ill|Taki District, Mie|en|Taki District, Mie|WD=Q1042360|lt=Taki District}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Forming a small village, it was established in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[:en:Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''{{ill|Shinden-zukuri|en|Shinden-zukuri|WD=Q2975225|lt=Shinden-zukuri}}'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after {{ill|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|en|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|WD=Q728218|lt=Dazaifu}}. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from {{ill|Saikū Station|en|Saikū Station|WD=Q7400129|lt=Saikū Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yamada Line|Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|WD=Q20983066|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == {{commonscat-inline}} *[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/ Saiku Historical Museum] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.itukinomiya.jp Itsukinomiya Historical Experience Hall ] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.town.meiwa.mie.jp/main/soshiki/saikuuato/bunkazaik/saiku/1455610636852.html Meiwa Town home page] {{in lang|ja}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Saikū]][[es:Saikū]][[fa:سایکو (محوطه تاریخی)]][[fr:Saikū]][[ja:斎宮]][[sh:Saikū]][[zh-classical:齋宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Saikū]][[es:Saikū]][[fa:سایکو (محوطه تاریخی)]][[fr:Saikū]][[ja:斎宮]][[sh:Saikū]][[zh-classical:齋宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Saikū]][[es:Saikū]][[fa:سایکو (محوطه تاریخی)]][[fr:Saikū]][[ja:斎宮]][[sh:Saikū]][[zh-classical:齋宮]]
{{Italic title|reason=
}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|WD=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa, Mie}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} through {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|WD=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa}}, {{ill|Taki District, Mie|en|Taki District, Mie|WD=Q1042360|lt=Taki District}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Forming a small village, it was established in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[:en:Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''{{ill|Shinden-zukuri|en|Shinden-zukuri|WD=Q2975225|lt=Shinden-zukuri}}'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after {{ill|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|en|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|WD=Q728218|lt=Dazaifu}}. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from {{ill|Saikū Station|en|Saikū Station|WD=Q7400129|lt=Saikū Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yamada Line|Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|WD=Q20983066|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

In addition to the museum, a reconstructed {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|WD=Q20983066|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)}}

{{Italic title|reason=
}} {{Other uses|Saigū (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = ''Saikū'' | native_name = 斎宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Saiku Heian no Mori 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Reconstructed halls of the ''Saikū'' in Meiwa | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saikū | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34.541207|136.616836|type:landmark_region:JP_source:enwiki| display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|WD=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa, Mie|1=Meiwa, Mie}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara|1=Nara period}} through {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''''Saikū''''' |斎宮}} was a palace complex located in what is now the Takegawa neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|WD=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa|1=Meiwa, Mie}}, {{ill|Taki District, Mie|en|Taki District, Mie|WD=Q1042360|lt=Taki District|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Forming a small village, it was established in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} as the palace and public offices of the ''[[Saiō]]'', an unmarried Imperial princess who served at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] on behalf of the [[:en:Imperial house of Japan|emperor]], and fell into ruins in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1979.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/p0048200012.htm|title = 斎宮歴史博物館 Reduced-scale reconstruction of the Saikuryo}}</ref> The Saikū is also referred to as the "Bamboo Palace", '''''Saigū''''', '''''Itsuki no Miya''''', '''''Iwai no Miya''''' or '''''Imimiya'''''<ref>{{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]}}</ref>
==Overview== The Saikū was located approximately ten kilometers north-west of Ise Shrine, arguably the most significant {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in Japan. The Saikū was situated on the right bank of the Kushida River and its tributary, the Harai River, which flows into {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay|1=Ise Bay}}. The original layout covered an area measuring two kilometers east-to-west by 700 meters north-to-south, or 137 hectares. The town was built on a grid structure based on Chinese traditions and consisted of several large blocks of 120 metres in length, surrounded by high wooden walls. Inside each block were more than 100 buildings in the ''{{ill|Shinden-zukuri|en|Shinden-zukuri|WD=Q2975225|lt=Shinden-zukuri|1=Shinden-zukuri}}'' style, with varying size and purpose, built of [[hinoki|Japanese cypress]] in the method of the day, using interlocking blocks of wood to hold the structure together without nails. The buildings were rectangular in shape and built on poles dug into the ground, with a floor raised up to a meter from the ground. Some blocks contained a small well from which to draw water, or shrines or structures for food storage. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item05 - The room of Saiô.jpg|thumb|left|Display item the room of the ''Saiō'', in the Saikū Historical Museum]] Saikū reached its peak from the late {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early Heian period indicate that the ''Saiō'' had a staff of more than 500 people, including some 120 officials. The Saikū was thus much larger than most district government complexes around the country, and was often referred to as the second largest provincial settlement after {{ill|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|en|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|WD=Q728218|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu, Fukuoka}}. After the collapse of the Saiō system, the town reverted to a rice-farming village and the exact location of Saikū was lost. While digging foundations for new housing development in 1970, a large ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' horse was found, one of the largest found in Japan. The housing construction was halted and [[archaeological excavation]]s began, confirming the site as that of the ancient Saikū town. A large number of shards of various earthenware, green glazed pottery, and circular inkstones were found, followed by the foundations of many structures. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Although further excavations take place annually on a relatively small scale, with much of the Saikū site still untouched due to its vast size. As the ancient buildings were made of wood, which has long since disappeared, and the site was redeveloped several times over its long history, excavations can reveal several generations of buildings whose foundations must be matched together to form a view of the town's layout. Most focus is given to the early Heian period, when the town was at its peak in size and influence. The Saikū Historical Museum now stands on the site of the original discovery and is about a three-minute walk from {{ill|Saikū Station|en|Saikū Station|WD=Q7400129|lt=Saikū Station|1=Saikū Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yamada Line|Kintetsu Yamada Line]]. In addition to the museum, a reconstructed {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} residence, known as ''Itsukinomiya Hall of Historical Experience'', has been built next to Saikū train station. Itsukinomiya Hall is not a restoration of a former building, but a reconstruction based on existing ''shinden-zukuri'' buildings elsewhere in Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|WD=Q20983066|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)}}
